name: Jimmy Mundy
description: American jazz tenor saxophonist, arranger, and composer (1907-1983)
abstract: James Mundy (June 28, 1907 – April 24, 1983) was an American jazz tenor saxophonist, arranger, and composer, best known for his arrangements for Benny Goodman, Count Basie, and Earl Hines. Mundy died of cancer in New York City at the age of 75.

about_quotes.speaker: Budd Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: I had entered into a studio with Eddie Bearfield and Jimmy Mundy and myself, we were in a studio on 48th Street... and we called it BMJ, which is Bearfield, Mundy, Johnson, and we were going to try to set up an arranging and copying service

about_quotes.speaker: Quinn Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: In the saxophone section, as I say, we had Darnell Howard, Omer Simeon on lead sax, James Mundy on tenor sax, Cecil Irwin on tenor sax

about_quotes.speaker: Chico Hamilton
about_quotes.evidence: Jimmy Mundy organized a band and I got a call from him and he wanted me ­ - and I joined his band
about_quotes.doc_title: Chico Hamilton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Clark Terry
about_quotes.evidence: I said, “Basie, I got your players all in line.” ... I introduced him to him and to Jimmy and I said “now this cat here, in case Mundy and Bootwhip – James Mundy and we used to call him Bootwhip — ... in case they get too busy, I says, this cat can write, he can help him out.
about_quotes.doc_title: Clark Terry interviewed by Joe Williams and Michael Woods, Clinton, New York, May 19, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Harry Edison
about_quotes.evidence: I think Basie got musicians that were more -- they could read better... Because at that time Jimmy Mundy was writing and -- let's see, who else was writing? Don Redman did some writing.

about_quotes.speaker: John Malachi
about_quotes.evidence: It also had a tenor saxophone player and arranger whose name was Jimmy Mundy... Jimmy Mundy had written this very popular, or I would say famous tune of his called 'Cavernism'
